coredata.dc:title: Influence of MoS2-La/CQDs quantum dispersion on the tribological behaviors of PEG200

Hu, Kunhong
coredata.dc:description: Two kinds of the novelty lubricant additives (La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions) were synthesized via a simply hydrothermal approach. The dispersions were demonstrated a significant enhancement in the lubrication performance of polyethylene glycol (PEG200). A series of modern analysis technologies were used to elucidate the anti-wear and anti-friction mechanisms of dispersions in PEG200. Results demonstrated that dispersions markedly improved the lubrication performance of PEG200 at 25, 75 and 100 °C. PEG200 containing 0.3 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs demonstrated the greatest decrease in the friction coefficient and wear rate, with reductions by 66.3 % and 35.5 % at 100 °C, respectively. Friction and wear mechanisms are ascribed to the engagement of nanoparticle-active components in both dispersions, which facilitate the formation of boundary lubrication films. 1
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots
Friction mechanisms
First principles simulation

Characterization of friction tests and analysis methods
Analysis of the friction and wear mechanisms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to express their thanks to Mr. Wuhao Jiang and Mr. Maosheng Wen for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS, HRTEM images and 3D friction testing machine diagram. The financial support received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52075144), Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (2008085ME167), Anhui Provincial Department of Education University Research Project (2024AH051505) are gratefully acknowledged. The Talent Research Fund of Hefei University (21-22RC33) and the Open Project of Anhui Province Engineering Laboratory of Intelligent Demolition Equipment (APELIDE2021B003), the Open Project of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Critical Friction Pair for Advanced Equipment (LCFP-2404) and Excellent Scientific Research and Innovation Team of Anhui University (2022AH010096) are also gratefully acknowledged.

The authors wish to express their thanks to Mr. Wuhao Jiang and Mr. Maosheng Wen for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS, HRTEM images and 3D friction testing machine diagram. The financial support received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52075144), Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (2008085ME167), Anhui University Outstanding Young Talents Programs (gxyqZD2020051) are gratefully acknowledged. The Talent Research Fund of Hefei University (21-22RC33) and the Open Project of Anhui Province Engineering Laboratory of Intelligent Demolition Equipment (APELIDE2021B003), the Open Project of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Critical Friction Pair for Advanced Equipment (LCFP-2404) and Excellent Scientific Research and Innovation Team of Anhui University (2022AH010096) are also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs quantum dot dispersions (a) La/CQDs nano-dispersion; (b) MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion; (c) The mutual solubility and fluorescence of CQDs dispersions and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions at 0.1 wt% mass concentration in PEG200.
TEM and particle size distributions for (a, b) La/CQDs and (c, d) MoS2-La/CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions, (b) Infrared spectra (IR) of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs (c) UV–visible (UV–vis) spectra of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions.
A four-ball friction tester and analysis method of friction and wear mechanisms.
Schematic diagram of the interface model
(a) C/Fe(110), (b) graphene/Fe(110), (c) La/CQDs/Fe(110), (d) La2O3/Fe(110), (e) MoS2/Fe(110), (f) MoS2-C/Fe(110) and (g) MoO3/Fe(110).
Variation in average friction coefficients for La/CQDs particles and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions with varying mass fractions, assessed under different temperature conditions
Variation in average wear spot diameter for La/CQDs particles and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions with varying mass fractions, assessed under different temperatures in condition

Surface morphology and energy spectrum analysis of worn surface of steel balls, along with the elemental distribution of MoS₂-La/CQDs particles, lubricated with varying concentrations of La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions.

Elemental valence analysis of wear traces of steel balls lubricated with different additive content of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions at the load of 392 N and sliding speed of 1200 rpm/min for 30 min.
First-principles simulation the adsorption energies of various substances on the crystal faces of bcc-Fe(110).
Raman spectra of the steel ball surface under lubrication with La/CQDs nanoparticles and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions.
PB values of solution samples with varying concentrations of MoS2-La/CQDs additives.
Contact surface stress of samples under various operating conditions.
SEM analysis of wear zones of steel balls at load 392 N and velocity 1200 rpm/min for 30 min for different temperatures.
EDS analysis of wear zones of steel discs and balls at load 392 N and velocity 1200 rpm/min for 30 min.

head.ce:title: Influence of MoS2-La/CQDs quantum dispersion on the tribological behaviors of PEG200

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, Anhui, Hefei, 230601, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two kinds of the novelty lubricant additives (La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions) were synthesized via a simply hydrothermal approach. The dispersions were demonstrated a significant enhancement in the lubrication performance of polyethylene glycol (PEG200). A series of modern analysis technologies were used to elucidate the anti-wear and anti-friction mechanisms of dispersions in PEG200. Results demonstrated that dispersions markedly improved the lubrication performance of PEG200 at 25, 75 and 100 °C. PEG200 containing 0.3 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs demonstrated the greatest decrease in the friction coefficient and wear rate, with reductions by 66.3 % and 35.5 % at 100 °C, respectively. Friction and wear mechanisms are ascribed to the engagement of nanoparticle-active components in both dispersions, which facilitate the formation of boundary lubrication films. 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The implementation of China's "14th Five-Years Plans" has catalyzed a surge in the development of high-end equipment and heightened demand for high-performance lubricants. This trend has also spurred an influx of scientists focusing on developing the innovative, eco-friendly, multi-functional lubricant additives. As is well known, it is widely recognized that lubricants are comprised of base oils and additives, with the latter playing a predominant role in enhancing lubricant performance. Additives are crucial for minimizing friction and wear, oxidation resistance, improving load-carrying capacity, and ensuring the reliability and longevity of machinery and equipment under harsh operating conditions [1]. Nanoparticles, when used as lubricant additives, exhibit the better friction-reduction and anti-wear properties, positioning them as a promising alternative to traditional additives while aligning with environmentally sustainable criteria [2]. Over recent decades, nanoparticles have been utilized as lubricant additives to improve the tribological characteristics of base oils, thereby mitigating wear and friction. Consequently, this prolongs the service life of sliding components and ensures the operational stability of machineries [3]. It is well-established that substantial energy loss in industrial processes results from friction and wear. Effective lubrication can conserve approximately 30 % energy. It is imperative to develop the innovative, efficiency and environmentally friendly lubrication additives.

Due to the small scale of nanomaterials, they were employed as lubrication additives, which readily infiltrated into the friction contact area. These materials are adept to forming a kind of protective tribo-film, thereby safeguarding the integrity of the friction sub-surface against wear. Nano-lubrication additives can be classified into two fundamental categories: nano-based additives and their composite additives [4]. Ag, Fe, Ni, and Cu nanoparticles are commonly utilized nanomaterials in the formulation of nano-lubrication additives. Zhang et al. [5] incorporated dialkyl dithiophosphate-modified copper nanoparticles into SJ15W/40 engine oil, using only 0.5 wt% of the nanoparticles leading to a reduction in wear rate by 90 % and a decrease in the coefficient of friction by 46 %. Huang et al. [6] functionalized the surface of calcium borate nanoparticles through the application of lauric acid. The carboxyl group of lauric acid exhibited a strong interaction with the surface of the nanoparticles. The presence of the terminal hydrocarbon group enables the calcium borate nanoparticles to achieve complete dispersion in organic solvents. Wang et al. [7] synthesized surface-modified zinc sulfide (ZnS) nanoparticles utilizing poly ethylene glycol monomethyl ether dithiophosphate (RPS) as a modifying agent. This modification improved the dispersion of ZnS nanoparticles and enhanced the stability of the nano-lubricant.

Carbon nanomaterials demonstrate better chemical stability and mechanical properties in comparison to metals and their composite additives. These attributes conferred the outstanding tribological performance and enhanced environmental sustainability, contributing to a more sustainable future. Nanocarbon-based lubricant additives consist of pure carbon forms and polymers derived from various chemical elements. These additives are categorized based on their dimensionality: Zero-dimensional (0D) form includes nano-diamonds and fullerene (C60). One-dimensional (1D) additives encompass carbon nanotubes; two-dimensional (2D) additives include graphene and three-dimensional (3D) consists of graphite [8]. Among them, Zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which consist of carbon (C) cores and surface groups, have garnered significant attention in lubrication due to their low toxicity and environmental friendliness [9]. CQDs are analogous to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10], displaying a uniform size distribution, good physicochemical properties, and ease of preparation and functionalization, while remaining safe and environmentally benign [11]. Tang et al. [12] found that 0.1 wt% CQDs substantially decreased the coefficient of friction and wear rate by approximately 33 % and 80 %, respectively.

Furthermore, the incorporation of doping elements or surface modifications can further enhance the frictional properties of CQDs [13]. Tu et al. [14] developed a novel green lubricant additive, nickel-doped carbon quantum dots (Ni-CQDs), using citric acid and nickel acetate. Both CQDs and Ni-CQDs were shown to enhance the lubricating properties of PEG200. Specifically, incorporating 2 wt% Ni-CQDs into PEG200 led to reductions in the average coefficient of friction and wear rate by 35.5 % and 36.4 %, respectively. Ma et al. [15] synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and ionic liquids (IL) using a direct modification approach, demonstrating good tribological properties. With a CQD content of 3.6 wt%, an ultra-low coefficient of friction (∼0.006) and a wear rate of approximately (∼0.7 × 10−14 m3/Nm) were attained.

These results offer valuable insights into the advancement of high-performance lubricants. MoS2 quantum dots exhibit the favourable lubricating properties, including high thermal and chemical stability, nanoscale dimensions, and favourable lubrication capabilities. Consequently, MoS2 quantum dot nanoparticles are extensively developed as additives for lubricants. Gong et al. [16] synthesized MoS2 quantum dots (with particle sizes of 10 nm) using an eco-friendly green synthesis approach and incorporated them into 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazole hexafluorophosphate ([BMIm]PF6), which maintains long-term dispersion stability. The results indicate that incorporating MoS2 quantum dots into the ionic liquid obviously enhances its load resistance and high-temperature performance. The components in the friction-induced ionic liquid phase form an effective lubricating film.

Rare earth elements are crucial to the advancement of China's high-tech industries and serve as key strategic resources in global competition [17]. China possesses the largest reserves of rare earth elements globally and plays a pivotal role in the international rare earth market [18]. As a critical national strategic resource, rare earth elements are extensively utilized in both civilian and defense advanced technologies [19]. To address China's strategic needs for rare earth metals and to develop high-performance lubricant additives, lanthanum-doped carbon quantum dots (La/CQDs) were synthesized for use as additives. Additionally, molybdenum disulfide quantum dot precursors were prepared via ultrasonic solvent-heat treatment and subsequently incorporated into the hydrothermal process of La/CQDs, resulting in the synthesis of a three components nano-dispersion (MoS2-La/CQDs).

To date, the tribological behavior of MoS2 quantum dots and lanthanum in the context of biomass carbon quantum dot lubrication has not been thoroughly investigated. In this paper, lanthanum chloride and citric acid were utilized as sources of lanthanum and carbon, respectively, to synthesize lanthanum-based carbon quantum dots. Subsequently, molybdenum disulfide, along with nitrogen, nitrogen-dimethylformamide, was employed as modifiers. MoS2 quantum dot precursors were prepared using an ultrasonication solvothermal method, followed by the synthesis of MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions via the solvothermal method. The tribological behavior of MoS2-La/CQDs nano-dispersions was evaluated using a four-ball tribo-meter, and a lubrication mechanism for these dispersions was proposed. The successful implementation of this research in utilizing rare earth elements for the development of high-performance lubricants establishes a foundational basis. Furthermore, it provides a fundamental guarantee for extending the service life of critical friction components in high-end equipment and enhancing their reliability in China.

A mixture of 0.21 mL ethanol (analytically grade, obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 30 mL of deionized water, 2.1 g citric acid (analytically grade, sourced from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), and 1.05 g lanthanum chloride (analytically grade, provided by Shandong Changyao Chemical Co., Ltd.) was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 2 h to achieve homogeneity. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a PTFE-lined hydrothermal kettle and heated at a constant temperature 180 °C for 5 h. Following the reaction, the solution was cooled to be the room temperature naturally before being filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate large particulates. It was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, dialyzed for 48 h using a 3500D dialysis bag, and freeze-dried to yield a homogeneous dispersion of La/CQDs. Fig. 1 (a) depicts the synthesized La/CQDs dispersion.
(2)
Preparation of MoS2-La/CQDs

2 g of MoS2 powder were combined with 200 mL of DMF (analytically grade, obtained from Aladdin) in a beaker. The mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 30 min to ensure thorough mixing and dispersion, followed by ultrasonic treatment using a 240 W ultrasonic bath (model KQ2200DB, supplied by Kunshan City Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.) for 6 h. The supernatant was decanted into a three-necked flask and subjected to vigorous stirring at 140 °C for 6 h in an oil bath. Following centrifugation and concentration, approximately 5 mL of MoS₂ quantum dot precursor was collected.

A mixture of 0.21 mL of ethanol (analytically grade, sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 30 mL of deionized water, 2.1 g of citric acid (analytically grade, obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 1.05 g of lanthanum chloride (analytically grade, supplied by Shandong Changyao Chemical Co., Ltd.), and 5 mL of MoS₂ quantum dot precursor was prepared. The solution was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 2 h to ensure homogeneous mixing before being transferred to a PTFE-lined hydrothermal reactor. The solution was then transferred to a PTFE-lined hydrothermal reactor and maintained at a constant temperature of 180 °C for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the solution was allowed to be cooled naturally to the room temperature. It was then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate large particulates, followed by high-speed centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed for 48 h using a 3500 Da dialysis bag and freeze-dried to yield homogeneous MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions. The synthesized MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersion is depicted in Fig. 1(b).
(3)
Dispersibility

The dispersions of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs were incorporated into the base oil PEG200 (purity 99 %, obtained from Shandong Yusuo Chemical Co., Ltd.), and the nanoparticle dispersion characteristics in PEG200 at varying mass fractions are illustrated in Fig. 1(c). The figure demonstrates that the MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion exhibits good stability in PEG200, with no observable precipitation occurring at room temperature for three months. The figure illustrates that MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion demonstrates good stability in PEG200, with no precipitation observed at room temperature after three months. This result signifies the successful implementation of the anti-settling strategy. Fig. 1(c) depicts the fluorescence characteristics of PEG200 and PEG200 containing La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs under 360 nm UV light. The fluorescence observed in the latter confirms the successful synthesis of quantum dots. The yield of the MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion was approximately 69 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical property of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs nanoparticles in dispersions
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai 200 kV) was employed to elucidate the internal structure and morphological features of La/CQDs particles in the dispersion, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (ãb). The La/CQDs particles exhibited uniform dispersion. A minimum of 100 particles were conducted using Nano Measurer software to determine the particle size distribution. This analysis revealed that La/CQDs nanoparticles predominantly ranged from 4.55 to 14.45 nm in size, with a uniform particle size and an average diameter of approximately 9.5 nm. TEM was employed to examine the internal structure and morphology of the MoS2-La/CQDs particles in the dispersions. Ethanol served as the dispersant, and the samples were subjected to be diluted followed by sonication for 15 min.

The results, depicted in Fig. 2(c) and (d), reveal that the MoS2-La/CQDs particles are uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 4.04 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, EI) at room temperature. Excitation wavelengths of 360 and 430 nm were employed for La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs, respectively. These spectra are illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). The maximum emission wavelength of La/CQDs fluorescence was observed at 461 nm, and this value remained stable over time without exhibiting any blue or red shift. The maximum emission wavelength for La/CQDs fluorescence is 461 nm and remains unchanged over time, without any observable blue or red shifts. In contrasting, the fluorescence emission wavelength for MoS2-La/CQDs is observed at 454 nm. The absorption spectra of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs were obtained using a UV–visible spectrometer (UV-1800, Mapada).

As depicted in Fig. 3(c), both La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs exhibit absorption primarily within the UV region. Notably, a broad absorption peak observed between 280 and 340 nm corresponds to the near-ultraviolet region. This peak suggests the presence of π→π∗ (C-C) and n→π∗ (C=O) transitions, indicative of unsaturated organic moieties such as -C=C- and related structures. The observed absorption features correspond to π→π∗ (C-C) and n→π∗ (C=O) electronic transitions. These transitions are indicative of the presence of unsaturated organic compounds, including -C=C-, as well as heteroatomic groups such as -C=O and -C=N- [20,21].

MoS2-La/CQDs exhibit intense bright green fluorescence when dissolved in PEG200, and appear light brown under natural light conditions. The molecular structure of material was analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet iS 50+ Continuum, Thermo Fisher, USA). Functional group absorption peaks were deduced from the vibrational modes of the molecular groups, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b). The absorption peak at 3450 cm⁻1 corresponds to -OH groups, while the peaks at 2655 cm⁻1 and 2825 cm⁻1 are attributed to antisymmetric stretching vibrations of -CH₂ and -CH₃ groups, respectively. The stretching frequencies observed at 1216, 1440, and 1730 cm⁻1 confirm the presence of -CH₂, -CH₃, and C=O groups, respectively [22–24]. The presence of these functional groups suggests that the synthesized carbon dots exhibit good water solubility, thereby demonstrating pronounced polar characteristics and facilitating effective intercalation with PEG200. Fig. 3(b) illustrates FTIR spectra of MoS2-La/CQDs, highlighting various functional groups at 3414 cm−1 (OH-stretching vibration), 1092 cm−1, 1024 cm−1 (C-O stretching vibration), 1662 cm−1 (C=O stretching vibration), 1385 cm−1 (CH3 stretching vibration), and 2469 cm−1 (-S-H stretching vibration) [25,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of friction tests and analysis methods
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of the two dispersions on the lubricity of PEG200 was assessed using a four-ball friction tester (MRS-10D), adhering to the SH/T0189-92 standard. Fig. 4 presents the sketch of tribo-meter and the analysis methods of friction and wear mechanisms. Testing was conducted under conditions of 392 N and 1200 rpm, at three temperature levels (25, 75, and 100 °C) for 30 min [27]. The maximum seizure-free load values (PB values, with a precision of ±10 N) for oils with varying concentrations of MoS2-La/CQDs additive solutions were determined according to GB/T 3142-2019 [28] and are summarized in Table 1 , along with PB values for different additive concentrations [29,30]. ASTM E2100-grade bearing steel, conforming to the Chinese national standard GB/T308-2002, was utilized. The steel balls feature a diameter of 12.7 mm, a hardness of HRC 65, and an average surface roughness of Ra 0.012 μm [31]. The experiments were performed under standard conditions, initiating with a maximum Hertzian contact pressure of 3.93 GPa [32].

The contact surface stresses between the steel balls during lubrication (where W denotes the pressure between the balls and a represents the circular contact area) were determined using the formula σ = 1.5 × W/(π × a2) [33], as detailed in Table 2 . This calculation indicates that MoS2-La/CQDs were all in a boundary lubrication regime when applied in PEG200.

To assess the lubricating properties of MoS2-La/CQDs as additives under diverse operational conditions, the samples were subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min prior to each experimental run. Furthermore, the test balls and oil cartridges were meticulously cleaned using petroleum ether. Each experimental series was conducted independently in triplicate to reduce statistical errors [34]. The morphology and elemental composition of the fixed ball abrasion marks were analyzed using SEM/EDS, while the diameter of the abrasion spots was also assessed with the SEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 model) was employed to investigate the valence states of the chemical elements, while Raman spectroscopy was utilized to analyze the elemental and phase composition of the friction film on the surface of the stationary spheres.

Adsorption energy and differential charge variations of friction products at the sub-interface are calculated using first-principles simulations, revealing insights into the friction wear mechanism. The detailed numerical simulation parameters and procedures are as follows: Three typical interfacial models using partial fragments of Graphene, La2O3, MoS2, and MoO3, including C/Fe(110), Graphene/Fe(110), La/CQDs/Fe(110), La2O3/Fe(110), MoS2/Fe(110), MoS2-C/Fe(110), and MoO3/Fe(110) (see Fig. 5 ). The MoS2 and La models employ a three-layer ideal bcc Fe(110) clean surface and a monolayer of graphene, respectively. To eliminate periodicity effects, a 15 Å vacuum layer was incorporated in all models. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were carried out using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP), applying the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) type of generalized gradient approximation (GGA). The structural optimization force criterion was set to 0.01 eV/Å per atom. For the C/Fe(110), Graphene/Fe(110), La/CQDs/Fe(110), La2O3/Fe(110), MoS2/Fe(110), MoS2-C/Fe(110), and MoO3/Fe(110) interface models, a plane wave cutoff energy of 520 eV was used, with the Brillouin zone sampled by a 2 × 2 × 1 grid. To assess the adsorption interactions along the ideal Fe (110) surface in the out-of-plane direction, it is necessary to focus on the strength of the interfacial interaction, namely the adsorption energy Ead. This can be effectively achieved by
E ad = E X@Fe(001) – E Fe(001) - E x
where the subscripts X/Fe(001) denote the three distinct interface models, while X signifies the associated partial fragment. Consequently, EX/Fe(001), EFe(001) and EX represent the computed total energies for the X/Fe(001) interface, the 3-layer Fe(110) surface, and the isolated partial fragment model, respectively.

Fig. 6 illustrates the friction reduction properties of PEG200 including La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions at temperatures of 25, 75 and 100 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the friction coefficient of PEG200 diminishes with increasing concentrations of La/CQDs nanoparticle dispersion at 25 °C, achieving a maximum reduction of 48.9 %, indicating enhanced friction reduction property of PEG200.

Friction coefficients of PEG200 varies with different concentrations of La/CQDs nanoparticle dispersions at 75 and 100 °C, with reductions of 45.2 and 60.0 % for the addition of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs dispersion in PEG200, respectively. The dispersion containing 0.3 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion exhibited an obvious reduction in friction coefficient compared to PEG200, with a maximum reduction of 58.6 % at 25 °C. Friction coefficient of PEG200 with addition of 0.5 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion was decreased by 50.6 % relative to that of PEG200 at 75 °C, Friction coefficient exhibited a reduction of up to 66.3 % at 100 °C with the addition of 0.3 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion. These findings suggest that the lubrication properties of MoS2-La/CQDs oil samples, synthesized using MoS2 quantum dots as precursors, are substantially improved, as depicted in Fig. 6.

Fig. 7 depicts variation in the average wear spot diameters (AWSDs) under lubrication with different samples containing different mass fractions of La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions, evaluated at various temperatures. The AWSDs of PEG200 was 0.69 mm at 25 °C. With the incorporation of varying amounts of La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions, the AWSDs showed a slight decrease, reaching 0.48 mm with the addition of 0.05 wt% La/CQDs dispersion, corresponding to a reduction rate of 29.4 %. The AWSDs of PEG200 was 0.76 mm at 75 °C. The addition of 0.1 wt% MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersion led to a significant reduction in AWSDs. MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersion, when was introduced at concentrations of 0.05 wt% and 0.3 wt%, demonstrated improved anti-wear properties for La/CQDs dispersion, achieving a maximum reduction of 25.1 %. The average wear spot diameter (AWSD) of PEG200 was 0.99 mm at 100 °C. The diameter decreased to 0.58 mm with the incorporation of 0.05 wt% La/CQDs nanoparticle dispersion, representing a reduction rate of 40.9 %. When 0.3 wt% MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersion was added, the AWSD reduced to 0.64 mm, corresponding to a reduction rate of 35.5 %. However, the anti-friction properties of La/CQDs nanoparticles in dispersion tend to deteriorate as their concentration increases at elevated temperatures [35].

Additionally, thermally oxidized CQDs are susceptible to forming agglomerates under high-temperature operating conditions with the rising concentration. This behavior is attributed to the fact that, with increasing concentration of La/CQDs nanoparticles, the number of -COOH groups also rises, thereby enhancing hydrogen bonding interactions. The incorporation of MoS2-La/CQDs effectively mitigates agglomeration interactions. Consequently, as the concentration increases, enhanced anti-wear properties of La/CQDs nanoparticles facilitated by MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion. The results indicate that MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions enhance the anti-wear properties of base oils PEG200 and La/CQDs at elevated temperatures. The optimal concentration of La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs nano-dispersion additives was determined to be 0.3 wt% at 100oC. Furthermore, MoS2-La/CQDs nano-dispersions exhibit a good enhancement in anti-wear performance of PEG200 at elevated temperatures.

To enhance the investigation of the tribological properties of samples at the optimal additive concentration, optical wear zone measurements were analyzed utilizing a SEM/EDS. Table 3 shows the surface morphology of the abraded areas lubricated by PEG200 and optimal addition concentrations (0.1 wt% La/CQDs and 0.1 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs) dispersions at the different temperatures. At 25 °C, numerous extensive pits and deep grooves were observed on the surface of the steel balls, as highlighted by the red arrows in Table 3. These observations indicate that pure PEG exhibits limited efficacy in protecting the steel ball surfaces. Upon the addition of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs dispersion, there was a marked reduction in both the width and depth of the furrows on the surface of the steel balls.

Additionally, a decrease in the number of pits and the formation of less dense grooves was observed. Furthermore, there was a substantial reduction in the cross-sectional length and wear area of the abrasion marks on the rotating ball. An appreciable amount of oil-like substance was observed on the surface of wear zones. The diameter of the abrasion spots on the steel ball was diminished compared to pure PEG lubrication, which indicating that La/CQDs nanoparticle dispersion exhibited good antifriction properties. Upon the addition of 0.1 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion, there was a marked reduction in the width of the surface abrasion marks on the steel balls of the upper specimen. Additionally, a considerable amount of abrasive debris and a minimal quantity of furrows were present on the surface. The diameter of the abrasion spots on the steel balls was markedly smaller compared to those observed with pure PEG lubrication. Furthermore, the friction film, indicated by the red arrow, is prominently visible on the surface. This film is identified as the primary factor contributing to the observed reduction in both friction coefficient and wear rate.

A large number of furrows and a small amount of abrasive debris were present on the surface of steel balls lubricated with PEG200 with the deeper furrows present on the surface of the balls as the temperature increased to 75 and 100 °C. Upon the addition of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and 0.1 wt% MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions, the furrows on the upper surface of the specimens were reduced. Simultaneously, a substantial quantity of fine abrasive debris was observed, which is likely due to the friction-induced oxidation of lanthanum or molybdenum disulfide, persisting at the friction sub-interface. Additionally, a considerable oil-like friction film was formed.

Fig. 8 and Table 4 illustrate the interfacial morphology, elemental composition, and distribution on the wear zones of the steel balls lubricated with PEG200 with the addition of 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs. The figure reveals that La (0.19 wt%) is present on the surface of steel balls lubricated with La/CQDs dispersion. In contrast, steel balls treated with MoS₂-La/CQDs quantum dots exhibit La (0.87 wt%) and Mo (0.33 wt%), with these elements being uniformly distributed at the wear zone interface. These results suggest that the incorporation of La and MoS2 facilitates the formation of boundary lubrication films as a result of frictional processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the friction and wear mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further elucidate the friction and wear mechanisms of La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions in PEG200. XPS was employed to analyze the chemical valence of elements on the surface of wear zones lubricated with different samples.

Fig. 9 (a–c) illustrate the C1s spectra for the lubrication interfaces of various samples. The three peaks observed in the C1s spectra, located at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.4 eV, correspond to the chemical bonds of C-C, C-O-C, and O=C-C, respectively. A comparative analysis of the three graphs reveals a significant content increase in the C-O-C and C-C=O groups on the wear zones of steel balls lubricated with 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersions. The results suggest that friction facilitated the involvement of components of dispersions to formation of boundary lubrication films [36,37]. Fig. 11(d–f) displays the O1s spectra of wear zones of steel balls. The spectra identify that the peak at 529.8 eV belongs to C-O, peak at 531.5 eV belongs to H-O-C, and peak at 533.1 eV C-O. As depicted in Figure (e), the peak at 531.5 eV is broader, likely due to the presence of La/CQDs organic compounds. The high peak at 533.1 eV is attributed to MoS₂-La/CQDs-promoted oxides, as shown in Figure (f). The characteristic peak of O1s for PEG 200, observed at 530 eV, falls between 529.8 eV and 533.1 eV, suggesting a combined effect of C-O and H-O-C interactions [38,39].

Fig. 9(g–i) present the Fe2p spectra of the friction interface following the incorporation of various quantum dots dispersions for lubrication. Significant differences in iron oxide content of sub-interface during friction process. Specifically, the peaks observed at 710.8 eV, 713.6 eV, 718.7 eV, and 724.5 eV correspond to the Fe 2p₃/₂ orbital [40–42]. Fe 2p₃/₂, Fe(II) 2p₃/₂, and Fe(III) 2p₃/₂ were identified on the wear marks. This observation can be ascribed to the boundary lubrication film, which incorporates MoS₂-La/CQDs particles formed during the friction process.

Fig. 9(j and k) displays the S2p and Mo3d valence spectra at the steel ball interface lubricated with PEG200 including the 0.1 wt% MoS₂-La/CQDs dispersion. The peak at 232.4 eV corresponds to the Mo 3d orbital of MoS₂, while the weak peaks at 231.9 eV and 235.5 eV are associated with the Mo 3d orbital of MoO₃. Peaks for the S element at 168.7 eV and 169.9 eV are attributed to the S2p orbital of MoS₂. These phenomena can be attributed to the formation of boundary lubrication films containing MoS₂-La/CQDs particles during friction, where Mo and S are predominantly present as MoS₂ [43–45]. The formation of this film leads to a reduction in the content of singlet iron, subsequently weakening the peak intensity. The XPS results further substantiate that MoS₂-La/CQDs particles play a role in the formation of the lubricating films.

In the view of the analysis results mentioned above, which reveals that MoS₂-La/CQDs nanoparticles dispersion not only imparted effective lubrication properties but also markedly altered the chemical composition of the wear region. These nanoparticles substantially enhanced lubrication efficacy and wear performance by establishing a stable boundary lubrication film.

To further validate the friction adsorption properties of La/CQDs and MoS₂-La/CQDs on the surface of steel balls, numerical calculations were performed using the first-principles methods. Fig. 10
illustrates the adsorption capacity values of various elements and substances at the frictional sub-interface before and after friction. Fig. 10(e and f) reveal that the adsorption energies of La/CQDs and friction-induced product of La₂O₃ on the Fe(110) crystal surface differ markedly. Specifically, the adsorption capacity of La₂O₃ is lower than that of La/CQDs. Furthermore, La₂O₃, as a surface friction induced product, tends to accumulate at the frictional sub-interface, facilitating the formation of a boundary lubrication film that enhances anti-wear properties and reduces friction. Regarding to the adsorption behaviors of MoS₂ and the carbon atoms from carbon quantum dots during friction process was investigated, as shown in Fig. 10(h–j). Numerical simulations of the friction-induced decomposition of MoS₂ into molybdenum trioxide and iron sulfide revealed that the adsorption energies of these products were significantly lower. These results suggest that these friction-generated substances tend to adsorb readily onto the substrate interface, facilitating the formation of a lubricating film. The amorphous carbon atoms within the quantum dots tend to transform into graphite-like fragments under friction process. These fragments are effective in providing lubrication function [46]. Numerical simulations indicate that the adsorption energies of surface graphene-like components are lower than those of amorphous carbon particles. This suggests that the formation of graphitized carbon due to friction enhances lubrication. These findings further confirm that incorporating MoS2-La/CQDs into PEG200 effectively reduces friction coefficient and wear rate.

Fig. 11 showed Raman spectrum analysis of worn zones of steel balls lubricated with PEG200 including 0.1 wt% La/CQDs and 0.1 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions. It exhibits two distinct characteristic peaks at approximately 1350 cm⁻1 and 1580 cm⁻1, corresponding to the D and G of amorphous and graphitized carbon, respectively. Comparing the results, it is evident that La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs, when added to PEG200, engaged in frictional chemical reactions and were transferred to the interface of the friction sub-steel ball, forming a transfer film including the graphite-like carbon. The ratio of the intensity of the D and G peaks (ID/IG) can be used to evaluate the degree of graphitization. Generally, the smaller the ratio is, the higher the degree of graphitization was obtained. Fig. 11 reveals that the ID/IG ratio for the abraded region with MoS2-La/CQDs lubrication is approximately 0.78, while for CQDs lubrication it is around 0.83. Under MoS2-La/CQDs lubrication, the D to G peak intensity ratios in the abraded region are notably lower, indicating a higher degree of graphitization compared to La/CQDs lubrication. This indicates that MoS2-La/CQDs lubrication facilitates a change in the graphitization degree of the carbon quantum dots during the frictional chemistry reaction. The MoS2 quantum dots contribute to improve tribological performance and enhanced lubricating properties compared to La/CQDs [47].

La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions were successfully synthesized using a solvothermal method. Their tribological behaviors as lubricant additives were investigated using a four-ball tribo-meter, and the corresponding friction and wear mechanisms were elucidated. The main conclusions were drawn as following.
1)
The particle sizes of La/CQDs nanoparticles in dispersion varied from 4.55 to 14.45 nm, with a uniform distribution and an average diameter of approximately 9.5 nm. In contrast, the average particle size of the MoS2-La/CQDs in dispersion was 4.04 nm.
2)
Both La/CQDs and MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions notably enhanced the lubrication properties of poly(diethylene glycol) across a range of temperatures, outperforming PEG200. PEG200 with 0.1 wt% La/CQDs demonstrated the greatest reduction in the friction coefficient, achieving a 60.0 % decrease at 100 °C. Similarly, the incorporation of 0.3 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersion improved the friction-reducing properties of PEG, resulting in a 66.3 % reduction in friction coefficient. Regarding to the anti-wear performance compared to PEG200, the addition of 0.05 wt% La/CQDs and 0.05 wt% MoS2-La/CQDs dispersions obviously enhanced the anti-wear properties, reducing the wear scar diameters by 40.9 % and 29.9 %, respectively.
3)
The primary mechanism behind the enhanced lubricity of PEG200 with the two dispersions is the friction-induced integration of La, MoS2 quantum dots, and carbon quantum dots in the formation of the boundary lubrication film. First-principles calculations reveal that friction products, including graphene, C, La, and MoS2, are readily adsorbed at the interface of the friction pair, forming a boundary friction film. Lanthanum and molybdenum disulfide exhibits catalytic properties that enhance the formation of a boundary lubrication film with a graphite-like structure of carbon at the friction interface, improving anti-wear performance and reducing friction.

Xu Tan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xiuqian Yu: Investigation, Data curation. Wenyang Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Yifan Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Enzhu Hu: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xingkai Ding: Writing – review & editing, Software. Chuan Li: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Kunhong Hu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their thanks to Mr. Wuhao Jiang and Mr. Maosheng Wen for their assistance in using the SEM/EDS, HRTEM images and 3D friction testing machine diagram. The financial support received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52075144), Anhui Province Natural Science Foundation of China (2008085ME167), Anhui Provincial Department of Education University Research Project (2024AH051505) are gratefully acknowledged. The Talent Research Fund of Hefei University (21-22RC33) and the Open Project of Anhui Province Engineering Laboratory of Intelligent Demolition Equipment (APELIDE2021B003), the Open Project of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Critical Friction Pair for Advanced Equipment (LCFP-2404) and Excellent Scientific Research and Innovation Team of Anhui University (2022AH010096) are also gratefully acknowledged.
